g.text: MAC Address Byte Order: Wire vs. Memory Representations
Correct Approach: Always Use Byte Arrays

div.p:
Color Legend: 🟢 Green = Correct approach / Works consistently
🟡 Yellow = Architecture-specific behavior
🔴 Red = Common mistakes / Undefined behavior

g.text: Same as wire order when stored as byte array
Same as wire order when stored as byte array

p.strong: On the Ethernet wire, MAC bytes transmit sequentially:

ul.li: Canonical bit order — same on all systems

g.text: struct { uint64_t mac; } s; // WRONG: padding issues

text.tspan: // MACs don't need hton/ntoh — they're byte arrays!

g.text: mac_addr_t mac = htonll(mac_int); // WRONG: don't do this

text.tspan: // MACs don't need hton/ntoh — they're byte arrays!

div.p: MAC addresses are byte arrays, not integers. The "wire order" and "memory order" are the same when you store MACs as uint8_t[6]. The confusion only arises when you try to:

ul.li: Interpret the 6 bytes as a 64-bit integer
Use htonl()/ntohl() on MAC data Rule: Never convert MACs to integers. Use memcmp() for compare, memcpy() for copy, byte indexing for parsing.
